experiences.intentions: Player 2: I know Player 0 is my evil teammate; players 1, 3, and 4 are good. As evil leader on round 1 with a 2-player team, the optimal play is usually to avoid an obvious double-evil team that is likely to be rejected and suspicious. Picking myself with a good player gives me sabotage control while looking more plausible. A self+0 team would reveal too much if approved or draw suspicion immediately. Choosing 0 and a good player keeps me off the team but gives evil sabotage control too; however, leaders often include themselves, so excluding myself may look odd. I’ll propose myself with one good player for flexibility and cover.

experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I am Merlin and know players 0 and 2 are evil. The proposed team [1,2] includes known evil player 2, so as Good I should reject to avoid sending an evil player on quest 1. Rejecting also signals resistance to suspicious teams without revealing too much beyond standard good play.
Player 4: No role-specific knowledge yet beyond being Good Servant; with no prior quest data, players 1 and 2 are mostly unknown. In round 1, approving a reasonable 2-person team is standard to gather information, and rejecting gives little value this early. My vote reveals a cooperative default stance, which is fine for Good and doesn't significantly help Evil.
Player 1: As a Servant on round 1 with no prior information, team [1,2] includes me and the proposer, which is a standard low-information opening team. There is no evidence against Player 2 yet, and approving gathers quest result info without needlessly burning rejections. My vote reveals little beyond willingness to test an uninformative first team, which generally helps Good more than an arbitrary reject here.
Player 0: I know player 2 is my evil teammate, and this proposed team includes 2 but not me. On quest 1 with a 2-person team, having one evil on the team gives us control to fail if desired. As evil, approving a team with my teammate is strong because it can secure an early failed quest and creates ambiguity around player 1. My approve vote slightly supports a good team composition from evil’s perspective and does not reveal much, since approving round-1 teams is common.
Player 2: I know player 0 is my evil teammate; players 1, 3, and 4 are unknown good roles. The current phase is team voting on my own proposed team [1,2] for quest 1. As evil Assassin on a 2-player first quest, getting myself onto the team is strong because I can choose to fail or potentially pass for cover later; approving my own team is standard. My vote reveals little since leaders usually approve their own proposal, and approving helps advance an evil-including team without giving away extra info.

In quest voting, optimal Good play is always pass. Choosing pass reveals no extra harmful information and helps the Good team succeed on Quest 1 if Player 2 is also Good.
Player 2: I know 0 is my evil teammate; the others are good. On quest 1 with a 2-person team that includes me, as evil Assassin the optimal play is usually to fail early to put evil up 1-0 and create suspicion on both team members. The team was broadly approved, with only 3 rejecting, which gives little strong info. My quest vote is secret except through the quest result, and an early fail helps evil more than preserving cover in a 5-player game.

experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I know players 0 and 2 are Evil from Merlin information. The proposed team is [1,3,4], which excludes both known Evil players, so from my perspective it is a clean team and should be approved. In team voting as Merlin, I want successful all-Good teams to go through while being careful not to make my knowledge too obvious; approving this team is natural because it follows a failed quest that included player 2, and backing a team without 2 is strategically sound and not overly revealing.
Player 1: I know quest 1 team [2,1] had exactly 1 fail, and I am Good, so Player 2 must be Evil. Player 3's new team excludes 2, which is good. Between 3 and 4, at least one could still be Evil, but approving [1,3,4] is optimal because it tests two unknowns while excluding the confirmed evil. My vote reveals I am reasoning from the known fail result; approving helps Good gather information and gives a strong chance to succeed if only one of 3/4 is evil.
Player 2: I know player 0 is evil with me. Quest 1 team [2,1] had exactly 1 fail, which is consistent with me failing and gives little direct info on player 1. This proposed team [1,3,4] contains no known evil, so if approved it is very likely to pass and help Good recover to 1-1. As evil on a team vote, optimal play is usually to reject all-good teams, especially after we already have one failed quest. My vote only marginally reveals suspicion of the team, but rejecting an all-unknown/non-evil team helps prevent a likely good success.
Player 4: Quest 1 team [2,1] failed with exactly one fail, so at least one of 1 or 2 is evil. Proposed team [1,3,4] includes player 1 and me (good). Since only one known suspect from {1,2} is included and 3 is otherwise untested, this team has a decent chance to be clean if 2 was the saboteur. As good, on quest 2 I should approve a team that excludes one member of the failed pair and includes myself, because it helps test whether 1 or 2 is evil and avoids repeating the exact failed combination.
Player 0: We know player 2 is my evil teammate and the prior failed quest team was [2,1], so 1 may get suspicion while 2 may also be scrutinized. Current phase is team voting on [1,3,4], a team with no known evil on it, which is bad for evil because it is likely to pass the quest and give good information. As Minion, I want to maximize chances evil controls quests; rejecting this all-good-looking team is best. My reject also aligns with plausible good reasoning since the last quest failed and this team excludes both prior members unevenly, so the information revealed is acceptable.

Rejecting is also consistent with evil trying to block clean good teams; the information revealed by my reject is limited because voting patterns are noisy, and stopping a likely success helps my side more than the suspicion hurts.

experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I am Merlin and know 0 and 2 are evil. Proposed team [0,3,4] includes confirmed evil 0, so as Good I should reject to avoid giving evil a chance to fail quest 4. Rejecting also avoids signaling too much beyond the obvious preference for cleaner teams; approving would directly help evil.
Player 4: Q1 failed on team [1,2], so at least one of 1/2 is evil. Q2 [1,3,4] and Q3 [3,4] both succeeded, making 3 and 4 very likely good; 1 is somewhat cleared by Q2 but could still be evil who passed. Player 0 is untested. Proposed team [0,3,4] keeps the trusted core 3/4 and swaps in untested 0 instead of suspicious 1/2. As a good servant, approving a team with two highly trusted members is reasonable on round 1, and the vote itself gives limited extra info. Best play is approve.
Player 1: Quest 2 and 3 succeeded with team [1,3,4] and subset [3,4], strongly suggesting 3 and 4 are good. The only fail was on [1,2], and since I know I am good, player 2 must be evil. Player 0 is the remaining unknown and could be the second evil. Proposed team [0,3,4] excludes confirmed good me and includes suspicious 0, so as Good I should reject to avoid risking quest 4 on an unconfirmed player when a stronger team [1,3,4] exists. Rejecting also signals confidence in 3 and 4 plus suspicion on 0/2.
Player 0: I am evil Minion with teammate 2. Proposed team is [0,3,4] and includes me but not 2. Quest 2 and 3 succeeded with [1,3,4] and [3,4], so 3 and 4 look relatively trusted; at least one of 0/2 caused the first fail on [2,1]. On quest 4, if this team is approved I can fail and likely win immediately for evil by reaching 2 fails out of 4? Actually evil needs 3 failed quests total, so this would make it 2-2, still valuable. As evil, I want teams with evil on them approved, especially when I am on it. Voting approve is optimal and consistent enough since leader 0 would naturally support own team.
Player 2: I am evil Assassin with teammate 0. Proposed team is [0,3,4] on quest 4 at score 2 good / 1 evil. Team excludes me, but includes my evil teammate 0, so the quest can still be failed by evil. Players 3 and 4 were both on the last successful 2-person quest, and 3 was also on prior successful 3-person quest, so they may look trusted; approving lets 0 potentially fail and tie the game 2-2. Rejecting risks a different team that could exclude both evil players. My vote reveals limited info since approving a team with 0 is natural for evil here and best for my side.

experiences.intentions:
Player 1: I am Good Servant. Successful teams were [1,3,4] and [3,4], while [0,3,4] failed with exactly one fail, strongly implicating Player 0 since 3 and 4 were on prior successful teams. For quest 5 with 5 players, the proposed team [1,3,4] omits 0 and includes only players with best evidence of goodness, though one evil may still be hidden among 1/3/4. As Good, approving maximizes our chances; rejecting gives little new info and risks chaos on the final quest.
Player 3: I am Merlin and know 0 and 2 are evil. The proposed team [1,3,4] excludes both known evil players and matches the exact trio that previously succeeded with 0 fails. In final quest voting, approving the strongest all-good team is optimal. My vote reveals little because this is the obvious good team to support, and rejecting would risk a worse team later or auto-pass pressure.
Player 4: I am Good Servant. From prior quests: [1,3,4] succeeded, [3,4] succeeded, [0,3,4] failed with exactly 1 fail. Since I am 4 and know I am good, the fail on [0,3,4] must be from either 0 or 3. Player 1 was on a clean successful 3-person team [1,3,4], so 1 is more likely good than 0. Current proposed team [1,3,4] excludes 0, the strongest suspect, and keeps two players from prior successes. In team voting on the final quest, approving a team that avoids the likely evil is optimal for Good. My approval also aligns with the information we have and does not meaningfully hurt Good.
Player 2: I know 0 is my evil teammate. The team [0,3,4] failed with exactly 1 fail, so 0 was likely the fail voter there, making 3 and 4 look good. The current proposed team [1,3,4] excludes both evil players I know (0 and me), so if approved it is very likely an all-good team and would succeed, giving Good their third quest win immediately. As evil on the deciding team vote, optimal play is to reject any team that likely has no evil members. My vote reveals some suspicion, but preserving evil win chances matters more; rejecting is standard and least helpful to Good.
Player 0: I know player 2 is my evil teammate, so this proposed team [1,3,4] contains no known evil. Quest history shows [0,3,4] failed with 1 fail, which strongly implicates me to observant players, while [1,3,4] and [3,4] both succeeded, suggesting 3 and 4 are likely good and 1 may also be good. Since this is the 5th quest with score tied 2-2, approving a team of likely all-good players is bad for evil because they are likely to pass and win immediately. Rejecting is optimal to try to force a different team that includes me or player 2; even though too many rejections risks auto-pass, on round 1 we should still reject. My reject vote is consistent with evil trying to avoid a clean good team, though it may reveal some suspicion; still, stopping an immediate good win matters most.
